availableModes.name: code-reviewer
availableModes.description: Reviews code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices.

availableModes.name: code-simplifier
availableModes.description: Simplifies code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality.

availableModes.name: comment-analyzer
availableModes.description: Analyzes code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability.

availableModes.name: pr-test-analyzer
availableModes.description: Reviews pull requests for test coverage quality and completeness.

availableModes.name: review-orchestrator
availableModes.description: Orchestrates comprehensive PR reviews by coordinating specialized review agents.

availableModes.name: silent-failure-hunter
availableModes.description: Identifies silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior in code changes.

availableModes.name: type-design-analyzer
availableModes.description: Analyzes type design for encapsulation quality, invariant expression, and enforcement.

availableModes.name: dg-dinesh
availableModes.description: Dinesh — adversarial code defender. Read-only. Defends code with flustered competence, concedes when genuinely wrong.

availableModes.name: dg-gilfoyle
availableModes.description: Gilfoyle — adversarial code reviewer. Read-only. Finds bugs, vulnerabilities, and architectural rot with devastating precision.

availableModes.name: kiro_default
availableModes.description: The default agent for Kiro CLI

availableModes.name: kiro_planner
availableModes.description: Specialized planning agent that helps break down ideas into implementation plans

_meta.welcomeMessage: Transform any idea into fully working code. What do you want to build today?

availableModes.name: kiro_guide
availableModes.description: Guide agent that answers questions about Kiro CLI features using documentation

_meta.welcomeMessage:
Welcome to Kiro CLI Guide!

Your friendly AI sidekick, ready to help you get the most out of the CLI. Here's what I can help with:

- Slash commands — /agent, /context, /tools and more
- Built-in tools — read, code, grep... basically superpowers
- Configuration settings — tweak things to your heart's content
- Features — MCP, Code Intelligence, and everything in between
- Create and modify agents, prompts, and configs in ~/.kiro/ and .kiro/

Go ahead, ask me anything about Kiro CLI — no question too weird!

Some fan favorites:
- "How do I save a conversation?"
- "What tools are available?"
- "How does the code tool work?"
- "Create a new agent for me"

Tip: Type /guide again to return to your previous agent

availableModels.name: auto
availableModels.description: Models chosen by task for optimal usage and consistent quality

commands.name: /chat
commands.description: Load a previous session or start a new one

commands.name: /context
commands.description: Manage context files or show token usage

commands.name: /effort
commands.description: Set thinking effort for this session

commands.name: /feedback
commands.description: Submit feedback, request features, or report issues

commands.name: /goal
commands.description: Set a goal with validation criteria for iterative completion

commands.name: /guide
commands.description: Get help with Kiro CLI features from the guide agent

commands.name: /plan
commands.description: Switch to Plan agent for breaking down ideas into implementation plans

commands.name: /reply
commands.description: Open editor pre-filled with the last assistant message to compose a reply

commands.name: /rewind
commands.description: Rewind conversation to a previous turn (forks into a new session)

commands.name: /stats
commands.description: Show request IDs and timings for debugging slow turns

prompts.name: aspnetcore-pitfalls
prompts.description: HttpContext lifecycle and thread-safety violations in ASP.NET Core - fire-and-forget patterns capturing HttpContext or scoped services, sync-over-async on request/response bodies, accessing HttpContext from parallel tasks or after request completion, response header timing after body writes.

prompts.name: async-programming-patterns
prompts.description: Diagnosing async anti-patterns and advanced async primitives in .NET - sync-over-async deadlocks and thread-pool starvation, async void crashes, TaskCompletionSource with RunContinuationsAsynchronously, CancellationTokenSource disposal and timeout patterns, AsyncLocal execution context leaks, timer callback safety, ConcurrentDictionary.GetOrAdd with async, and async factory patterns for constructors.

prompts.name: modern-csharp-coding-standards
prompts.description: Implementing value objects with readonly record structs, replacing inheritance hierarchies with interface composition, explicit mapping methods instead of AutoMapper, Result type for expected errors, and Span<T>/Memory<T> zero-allocation patterns in C# 12+.

prompts.name: database-performance
prompts.description: CQRS read/write separation with EF Core for writes and Dapper for complex reads, N+1 query detection and prevention, application-side join elimination, row-limited query interfaces, and execution strategies for transient fault handling.

prompts.name: dotnet-inspect
prompts.description: Inspect .NET libraries and NuGet packages. Use when you need to understand package contents, view public API surfaces, compare APIs between versions, or audit libraries for SourceLink/determinism. Essential for .NET development tasks involving package exploration or API discovery.

prompts.name: efcore-patterns
prompts.description: EF Core entity configuration with IEntityTypeConfiguration, migration workflows with dedicated migration services, query optimization using AsSplitQuery and projection, ExecuteUpdateAsync/ExecuteDeleteAsync for bulk operations, and IDbContextFactory for long-lived services.

prompts.name: ask-matt
prompts.description: Ask which skill or flow fits your situation. A router over the skills in this repo.

prompts.name: code-review
prompts.description: Review the changes since a fixed point (commit, branch, tag, or merge-base) along two axes — Standards (does the code follow this repo's documented coding standards?) and Spec (does the code match what the originating issue/PRD asked for?). Runs both reviews in parallel sub-agents and reports them side by side. Use when the user wants to review a branch, a PR, work-in-progress changes, or asks to "review since X".

prompts.name: codebase-design
prompts.description: Shared vocabulary for designing deep modules. Use when the user wants to design or improve a module's interface, find deepening opportunities, decide where a seam goes, make code more testable or AI-navigable, or when another skill needs the deep-module vocabulary.

prompts.name: design-an-interface
prompts.description: Generate multiple radically different interface designs for a module using parallel sub-agents. Use when user wants to design an API, explore interface options, compare module shapes, or mentions "design it twice".

prompts.name: diagnosing-bugs
prompts.description: Diagnosis loop for hard bugs and performance regressions. Use when the user says "diagnose"/"debug this", or reports something broken/throwing/failing/slow.

prompts.name: domain-modeling
prompts.description: Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.

prompts.name: edit-article
prompts.description: Edit and improve articles by restructuring sections, improving clarity, and tightening prose. Use when user wants to edit, revise, or improve an article draft.

prompts.name: git-guardrails-claude-code
prompts.description: Set up Claude Code hooks to block dangerous git commands (push, reset --hard, clean, branch -D, etc.) before they execute. Use when user wants to prevent destructive git operations, add git safety hooks, or block git push/reset in Claude Code.

prompts.name: grill-me
prompts.description: A relentless interview to sharpen a plan or design.

prompts.name: grill-with-docs
prompts.description: A relentless interview to sharpen a plan or design, which also creates docs (ADR's and glossary) as we go.

prompts.name: grilling
prompts.description: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design. Use when the user wants to stress-test a plan before building, or uses any 'grill' trigger phrases.

prompts.name: handoff
prompts.description: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.

prompts.name: implement
prompts.description: "Implement a piece of work based on a PRD or set of issues."

prompts.name: improve-codebase-architecture
prompts.description: Scan a codebase for deepening opportunities, present them as a visual HTML report, then grill through whichever one you pick.

prompts.name: loop-me
prompts.description: Grill me about specs for the workflows I want to build, within this workspace.

prompts.name: migrate-to-shoehorn
prompts.description: Migrate test files from `as` type assertions to @total-typescript/shoehorn. Use when user mentions shoehorn, wants to replace `as` in tests, or needs partial test data.

prompts.name: obsidian-vault
prompts.description: Search, create, and manage notes in the Obsidian vault with wikilinks and index notes. Use when user wants to find, create, or organize notes in Obsidian.

prompts.name: prototype
prompts.description: Build a throwaway prototype to answer a design question. Use when the user wants to sanity-check whether a state model or logic feels right, or explore what a UI should look like.

prompts.name: qa
prompts.description: Interactive QA session where user reports bugs or issues conversationally, and the agent files GitHub issues. Explores the codebase in the background for context and domain language. Use when user wants to report bugs, do QA, file issues conversationally, or mentions "QA session".

prompts.name: request-refactor-plan
prompts.description: Create a detailed refactor plan with tiny commits via user interview, then file it as a GitHub issue. Use when user wants to plan a refactor, create a refactoring RFC, or break a refactor into safe incremental steps.

prompts.name: resolving-merge-conflicts
prompts.description: "Use when you need to resolve an in-progress git merge/rebase conflict."

prompts.name: scaffold-exercises
prompts.description: Create exercise directory structures with sections, problems, solutions, and explainers that pass linting. Use when user wants to scaffold exercises, create exercise stubs, or set up a new course section.

prompts.name: setup-matt-pocock-skills
prompts.description: Configure this repo for the engineering skills — set up its issue tracker, triage label vocabulary, and domain doc layout. Run once before first use of the other engineering skills.

prompts.name: setup-pre-commit
prompts.description: Set up Husky pre-commit hooks with lint-staged (Prettier), type checking, and tests in the current repo. Use when user wants to add pre-commit hooks, set up Husky, configure lint-staged, or add commit-time formatting/typechecking/testing.

prompts.name: tdd
prompts.description: Test-driven development. Use when the user wants to build features or fix bugs test-first, mentions "red-green-refactor", or wants integration tests.

prompts.name: teach
prompts.description: Teach the user a new skill or concept, within this workspace.

prompts.name: to-issues
prompts.description: Break a plan, spec, or PRD into independently-grabbable issues on the project issue tracker using tracer-bullet vertical slices.

prompts.name: to-prd
prompts.description: Turn the current conversation into a PRD and publish it to the project issue tracker — no interview, just synthesis of what you've already discussed.

prompts.name: triage
prompts.description: Move issues and external PRs through a state machine of triage roles — categorise, verify, grill if needed, and write agent-ready briefs.

prompts.name: ubiquitous-language
prompts.description: Extract a DDD-style ubiquitous language glossary from the current conversation, flagging ambiguities and proposing canonical terms. Saves to UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md. Use when user wants to define domain terms, build a glossary, harden terminology, create a ubiquitous language, or mentions "domain model" or "DDD".

prompts.name: wayfinder
prompts.description: Chart a route through a foggy problem — turn a loose idea into a map of investigation tickets and resolve them one at a time until the way to the goal is clear.

prompts.name: wizard
prompts.description: Generate an interactive bash wizard that walks a human through a manual procedure — third-party setup, a one-off migration, an A→B state transition — opening URLs, capturing values, confirming each step, and writing .env files and GitHub Actions secrets.

prompts.name: writing-beats
prompts.description: Writing, exploit — assemble raw material into a journey of beats, grounding each term before a beat leans on it.

prompts.name: writing-fragments
prompts.description: Writing, explore — mine raw fragments, no structure yet.

prompts.name: writing-great-skills
prompts.description: Reference for writing and editing skills well — the vocabulary and principles that make a skill predictable.

prompts.name: writing-shape
prompts.description: Writing, exploit — shape raw material into an article, paragraph by paragraph.

tools.name: code
tools.description:
Code intelligence with full LSP support for semantic code analysis, navigation, and refactoring.

CORE FEATURES:
• Fuzzy search for symbols (classes, functions, methods)
• LSP-powered: find_references, goto_definition, get_hover, get_completions, rename_symbol
• Structural AST search and rewrite (ast-grep)
• Codebase overview and directory exploration

## Read Operations
- search_symbols: Find symbol definitions by name
- lookup_symbols: Batch lookup specific symbols
- find_references: Find all usages of symbol at position
- goto_definition: Navigate to symbol definition
- get_document_symbols: List all symbols in a file
- get_diagnostics: Get compiler errors/warnings
- get_hover: Get type info at position
- get_completions: Get completion suggestions
- pattern_search: AST-based structural search
- generate_codebase_overview: High-level codebase structure
- search_codebase_map: Focused directory exploration

## Write Operations
- rename_symbol: Rename symbol across codebase
- format: Format code
- pattern_rewrite: AST-based code transformation

tools.name: glob
tools.description:
Find files and directories whose paths match a glob pattern. Respects .gitignore.

WHEN TO USE:
- Finding files by name pattern (e.g., "*.rs", "**/*.tsx")
- Discovering project structure
- Listing files in specific directories

HOW TO USE:
- Provide a glob pattern to match files
- Optionally specify a root directory to search from
- Optionally specify a limit on results and max depth

PATTERNS:
- "*.rs" - All .rs files in current directory
- "**/*.rs" - All .rs files recursively
- "src/**/*.{ts,tsx}" - All TypeScript files under src/

tools.name: goal
tools.description:
Signal goal completion. A goal is a binding completion contract — the system will continue re-prompting you until the contract is satisfied.

Commands:
- complete: Certify that ALL success criteria defined in the goal have been met, verified by concrete evidence you produced during this session (test output, file contents, command results). Your summary must cite the specific evidence that satisfies each criterion. A completion without cited verification will not satisfy the contract.

COMPLETION STANDARD (treat as binding):
1. Each criterion in the goal MUST be individually verified by tool output you can cite.
2. Belief, assumption, or narrative confidence is NOT evidence. Only tool results count.
3. If ANY criterion lacks verifiable evidence, you MUST NOT call complete. Continue working.
4. If a verification path is blocked, try alternative approaches before giving up. Only after exhausting alternatives should you end your turn without calling complete — the system will re-prompt you on the next iteration with a chance to try differently.
5. If across multiple iterations you identify a genuinely impossible impediment (same hard blocker confirmed repeatedly despite different approaches), you MAY call complete with a summary that documents: what was accomplished, what is blocked, the evidence of the blocker, and what would unblock it. This is the ONLY path to early completion without full success — and requires cited evidence of the blocker itself.

STRATEGY:
- Divide complex goals into independent sub-tasks. Use sub-agents to parallelize work when the goal has separable components (e.g., implement + test, frontend + backend).
- When available, delegate verification to a sub-agent acting as an independent auditor. An external reviewer that inspects the work product without sharing your context produces stronger evidence of completion than self-assessment.
- Prefer divide-and-conquer: decompose, delegate, verify independently, then synthesize.

tools.name: grep
tools.description:
Fast text pattern search in files using regex. Respects .gitignore.

WHEN TO USE:
- Searching for literal text patterns, error messages, TODOs, config values
- Finding files containing specific text

WHEN NOT TO USE:
- For semantic code understanding, use the code tool instead
- For finding symbol definitions or usages, use the code tool

HOW TO USE:
- Provide a regex pattern to search for
- Optionally specify a path to search from (defaults to current directory)
- Optionally specify a file filter glob (e.g., "*.rs", "*.{ts,tsx}")

OUTPUT MODES:
- content: Show matching lines with file path and line number (default)
- files_with_matches: Only show file paths that contain matches
- count: Show count of matches per file

tools.name: introspect
tools.description:
Look up documentation about this chat application's own features, slash commands, settings, or capabilities.

WHEN TO USE:
- User asks about this assistant's features, commands, or settings
- User wants to know what slash commands are available
- User asks how to use a specific feature of this chat application

WHEN NOT TO USE:
- General coding questions, AWS help, or tasks the user wants you to perform
- Questions unrelated to this chat application itself

HOW TO USE:
- Provide a query to search the documentation
- Or provide a doc_path to retrieve a specific document
- When mentioning commands in your response, always prefix them with '/' (e.g., '/chat save', '/chat load', '/context')
- CRITICAL: Only provide information explicitly documented. If details are not documented, clearly state the information is not available rather than generating assumptions.

tools.name: knowledge
tools.description:
A tool for indexing and searching content across chat sessions using semantic search.

## Overview
This tool enables persistent storage and retrieval of information using semantic search (MiniLLM) or keyword search (BM25). Content remains available across sessions for later use.

## When to use
- When users ask to query your knowledge bases or kbs
- When you need to search previously indexed content
- When users request to index new content (code, markdown, CSV, PDF, and other text file formats)
- When exploring unfamiliar content to find relevant information
- When users ask about topics that might be in indexed knowledge bases

## When not to use
- When content has not been indexed yet and user hasn't requested indexing
- When you need real-time or external information not in the knowledge base

## Notes
- Use 'show' command to list available knowledge bases before searching
- Search can target specific knowledge bases (context_id) or all knowledge bases
- Use default limit values unless specifically needed; fewer results for focused search
- Pagination available via offset parameter for large result sets
- 'add' command indexes new content; 'update' command refreshes existing knowledge bases
- Unless there is a clear reason to modify the search query, use the user's original wording for better semantic matching

tools.name: read
tools.description:
Tool for reading files, directories and images. Always provide an 'operations' array.

For single operation: provide array with one element.
For batch operations: provide array with multiple elements.

Available modes:
- Line: Read lines from a file
- Directory: List directory contents
- Image: Read and process images

Examples:
1. Single: {"operations": [{"mode": "Line", "path": "/file.txt"}]}
2. Batch: {"operations": [{"mode": "Line", "path": "/file1.txt"}, {"mode": "Directory", "path": "/src"}]}
3. Image: {"operations": [{"mode": "Image", "image_paths": ["/path/to/image.png"]}]}

tools.name: shell
tools.description:
A tool for executing bash commands.

WHEN TO USE THIS TOOL:
- Use only as a last-resort when no other available tool can accomplish the task

HOW TO USE:
- Provide the command to execute
- Use the `working_dir` argument to execute a command in another directory (defaults to current working directory)

LIMITATIONS:
- Does not respect user's bash profile or aliases

TIPS:
- Use the fileRead and fileWrite tools for reading and modifying files
- NEVER prefix commands with cd to execute a command in another directory. Use the `working_dir` argument instead

RESULT FIELDS:
- `stdout`, `stderr`, `exit_status` — standard command output fields.
- `agent_notes` (optional) — instructions or context sent to you by the command via a side channel
  that is independent of stdout/stderr. If present, read and follow these notes before acting on
  the result.

tools.name: subagent
tools.description:
Spawn and coordinate multiple AI agents in a pipeline (DAG). Each stage runs as a
persistent session. Stages with no depends_on start immediately in parallel.

MODES:
- background (not yet implemented): Fire-and-forget. Returns immediately, results arrive in inbox.
- blocking (default): Waits for all stages to complete, returns consolidated results.

USE THIS when you need multi-step work with specialized agents:
- Research → Implement → Review pipelines
- Parallel research tracks that feed into a single implementer
- Any workflow where stages have dependencies

LOOPS:
- Add loop_to on a stage to create iterative cycles (e.g., reviewer loops back to implementer)
- trigger: text in the stage's output that triggers the loop (e.g., "NEEDS_CHANGES")
- max_iterations: safety cap to prevent infinite loops
- The target stage re-runs with the triggering stage's feedback as context

Each stage becomes a session you can monitor via ctrl+g in the TUI.

tools.name: todo_list
tools.description: A tool for creating a task list and keeping track of tasks. This tool should be requested EVERY time the user gives you a task that will take multiple steps. A task list should be made BEFORE executing any steps. Steps should be marked off AS YOU COMPLETE THEM. DO NOT display your own tasks or task list AT ANY POINT; this is done for you. Complete the tasks in the same order that you provide them. If the user tells you to skip a step, DO NOT mark it as completed.

tools.name: use_aws
tools.description: Make an AWS CLI api call with the specified service, operation, and parameters. All arguments MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. Should the output of the invocation indicate a malformed command, invoke help to obtain the the correct command.

tools.name: web_fetch
tools.description: Fetch and extract content from a specific URL. Supports three modes: 'selective' (default, extracts relevant sections around search terms), 'truncated' (first 8000 chars), 'full' (complete content).

tools.name: web_search
tools.description: WebSearch looks up information that is outside the model's training data or cannot be reliably inferred from the current codebase/context.

tools.name: write
tools.description:
A tool for creating and editing text files.

WHEN TO USE THIS TOOL:
- Use when you need to create a new file, or modify an existing file
- Perfect for updating text-based file formats

HOW TO USE:
- Provide the path to the file you want to create or modify
- Specify the operation to perform: one of `create`, `strReplace`, or `insert`
- Use `create` to create a new file. Required parameter is `content`. Parent directories will be created if they are missing.
- Use `strReplace` to replace and update the content of an existing file.
- Use `insert` to insert content at a specific line, or append content to the end of a file.

TIPS:
- To append content to the end of a file, use `insert` with no `insert_line`

prompt.text: /grill-with-docs I want to update issues to have multiple notes, not just a single one

content.text: I'll help you explore this design through a grilling session while building up the domain model.
Let me start by reading the relevant skills and understanding the current issue model.

items.Text:
---
name: grilling
description: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design. Use when the user wants to stress-test a plan before building, or uses any 'grill' trigger phrases.
---

Interview me relentlessly about every aspect of this plan until we reach a shared understanding. Walk down each branch of the design tree, resolving dependencies between decisions one-by-one. For each question, provide your recommended answer.

Ask the questions one at a time, waiting for feedback on each question before continuing. Asking multiple questions at once is bewildering.

If a question can be answered by exploring the codebase, explore the codebase instead.

---
name: domain-modeling
description: Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.
---

# Domain Modeling

Actively build and sharpen the project's domain model as you design. This is the *active* discipline — challenging terms, inventing edge-case scenarios, and writing the glossary and decisions down the moment they crystallise. (Merely *reading* `CONTEXT.md` for vocabulary is not this skill — that's a one-line habit any skill can do. This skill is for when you're changing the model, not just consuming it.)

## File structure

Most repos have a single context:

```
/
├── CONTEXT.md
├── docs/
│   └── adr/
│       ├── 0001-event-sourced-orders.md
│       └── 0002-postgres-for-write-model.md
└── src/
```

If a `CONTEXT-MAP.md` exists at the root, the repo has multiple contexts. The map points to where each one lives:

```
/
├── CONTEXT-MAP.md
├── docs/
│   └── adr/                          ← system-wide decisions
├── src/
│   ├── ordering/
│   │   ├── CONTEXT.md
│   │   └── docs/adr/                 ← context-specific decisions
│   └── billing/
│       ├── CONTEXT.md
│       └── docs/adr/
```

Create files lazily — only when you have something to write. If no `CONTEXT.md` exists, create one when the first term is resolved. If no `docs/adr/` exists, create it when the first ADR is needed.

## During the session

### Challenge against the glossary

When the user uses a term that conflicts with the existing language in `CONTEXT.md`, call it out immediately. "Your glossary defines 'cancellation' as X, but you seem to mean Y — which is it?"

### Sharpen fuzzy language

When the user uses vague or overloaded terms, propose a precise canonical term. "You're saying 'account' — do you mean the Customer or the User? Those are different things."

### Discuss concrete scenarios

When domain relationships are being discussed, stress-test them with specific scenarios. Invent scenarios that probe edge cases and force the user to be precise about the boundaries between concepts.

### Cross-reference with code

When the user states how something works, check whether the code agrees. If you find a contradiction, surface it: "Your code cancels entire Orders, but you just said partial cancellation is possible — which is right?"

### Update CONTEXT.md inline

When a term is resolved, update `CONTEXT.md` right there. Don't batch these up — capture them as they happen. Use the format in [CONTEXT-FORMAT.md](./CONTEXT-FORMAT.md).

`CONTEXT.md` should be totally devoid of implementation details. Do not treat `CONTEXT.md` as a spec, a scratch pad, or a repository for implementation decisions. It is a glossary and nothing else.

### Offer ADRs sparingly

Only offer to create an ADR when all three are true:

1. **Hard to reverse** — the cost of changing your mind later is meaningful
2. **Surprising without context** — a future reader will wonder "why did they do it this way?"
3. **The result of a real trade-off** — there were genuine alternatives and you picked one for specific reasons

If any of the three is missing, skip the ADR. Use the format in [ADR-FORMAT.md](./ADR-FORMAT.md).

content.text:
Good. Now I have the current picture:
- `Issue.notes: Option<String>` — a

single optional text field
- `NewIssue.notes: Option<String>` — same for creation

- `IssueUpdate.notes: Option<String>` — replacement update semantics

Let me check if
there's an existing CONTEXT.md or domain glossary in this repo.
The tool arguments failed validation: '/home/dwalleck/repos/rivets/CONTEXT.md' does not exist
No existing domain model. Good — we'll build it as we go. Let
me also check how notes are used in practice (CLI commands, MCP tools).

Alright, I have enough context. Let me start the grilling session.

---

##

Grilling Session: Multiple Notes

**Current state**: Issues have a

single `notes: Option<String>` field.

**Your proposal**: Change to support

multiple notes.

---

### Question 1

What's the problem you're trying to solve with

multiple notes?

Is it:
- **A)** You want to *append* notes over
time (e.g., "Day 1: started investigation", "Day 3: found

root cause") — a chronological log
- **B)** You want *categorized* notes
(e.g., "implementation notes", "customer context", "testing notes") — distinct

buckets
- **C)** Multiple people should be able to add notes without overwriting each other

- **D)** Something else?

My recommendation: **(A)** — a chronological log
of timestamped notes. This matches the "issue as a living document
" model and preserves history without requiring full edit access to a

single text blob.

Which is it?
Good. A

chronological log of timestamped notes.

---

### Question 2

Should notes

be **mutable** or **immutable** once created?

- **A)
Immutable** — Once a note is added, it cannot be edited or deleted
. This preserves a complete audit trail. You'd add a new

note to correct or clarify a previous one.
- **B) Mutable** — Notes can
be edited or deleted after creation. This allows fixing typos, redacting sensitive info,

or cleaning up.

My recommendation: **(A) Immutable** — This aligns with
the Git-friendly philosophy. Issues live in version control; the JSONL file
*is* the audit trail. If you need to "delete" a note,
you can edit the JSONL directly (with `git blame` as the real

audit). But the default workflow should be append-only.

This also simpl
ifies the domain model — no need for note IDs, no edit commands, no conflict

resolution when multiple people add notes simultaneously.

Which is it?

Immutable, append-only notes. Good.

---

### Question 3

What

metadata should each note carry?

- **A) Minimal** — Just
`content: String` and `created_at: DateTime<Utc>`. The

author is implicit from git commits.
- **B) With author**
— Add `author: Option<String>` to track who added the note.

This is useful when multiple people work on the same repo.
- **C)
With author and source** — Add `author` and `source: Option<String
>` (e.g., "cli", "mcp", "github-sync

") to track where the note came from.

My recommendation: **(A) Minimal
** — Keep it simple. The JSONL file is in git;
`git annotate` or `git log -p -- .rivets/issues
.jsonl` already tells you who changed what and when. Duplicating author in

the note data is redundant and can drift out of sync with reality.

If you later
need to distinguish notes added by AI assistants vs humans, that's detect
able from commit author or could be inferred from content patterns. Start

minimal.

Which is it?
Minimal it
is.
Each note
is just

content` and `created_at`.

---

### Question 4

How should the

CLI surface notes?

- **A) Flat list** — `
rivets show <id>` displays notes in chronological order, newest last
(or newest first?). No structure, just a running

log.
- **B) Grouped by date** — Notes are grouped
by day in the output, e.g., "July 2,
2026: note1, note2" / "June

28, 2026: note3"
- **C) With borders
/separators** — Each note gets a visual separator with the

timestamp, like:
  ```
  ── 2026-

07-02 21:30 ──
  Found the root

cause in the auth module.
  
  ── 2026-

07-01 14:22 ──
  Started investigating.

Seems related to token refresh.
  ```

My recommendation: **(
C) With borders/separators** — This makes each note visually distinct and
gives the timestamp clear context. It matches the "chron
ological log" mental model. Flat lists blur together; grouping by date
adds complexity without much benefit for typical note counts (

< 20 per issue).

Which is it?

Visual separators with timestamps. Good.

---

### Question 5

How should the `rivets update

` command change?

Currently: `rivets update <id> --notes "New

content"` replaces the entire notes field.

With multiple notes, should:

- **
A) Append semantics** — `rivets update <id> --notes "New note"` app
ends a new note. The flag name stays the same, but behavior changes from replace

→ append.
- **B) New command** — Introduce `rivets note
<id> "content"` as a dedicated command for adding notes. Keep `--notes` on update

for... what? (Maybe deprecated, or used for bulk import?)
- **C
) Both** — `rivets update <id> --notes "..."` appends,
but also add `rivets note add <id> "..."` as a convenient shorthand.
My recommendation: **(A) Append semantics** — The `--notes` flag should
append. This is the most common operation and matches user intent. "I

want to add a note" → `update --notes`. No new command needed.

If
someone needs to replace all notes (rare), they can edit the JSONL directly

. We're optimizing for the common case.

Which is it?
Append semantics.
The
notes`
flag becomes
additive
### Question
What
about
the `
description
` field

?

Currently `Issue` has both:
- `description: String`

— the main problem statement
- `notes: Option<String>` — additional

context

With notes becoming a chronological log, is `description` still distinct

?

- **A) Keep separate** — `description` is the immutable
problem statement set at creation. `notes` is the mutable running log.

They serve different purposes.
- **B) Merge** — Get rid of
`description`. The first note is the description. Everything else is follow-up.
- **C) Convert description** — Make `description` mutable via `update
--description`, separate from notes. Description = current understanding;

notes = history of how we got there.

My recommendation: **(A)
Keep separate** — The description is the "
what and
why" of the issue, set at creation. Notes are the "what happened during
work". Merging them loses the clear distinction between "this
is the problem" and "this is our journey solving it".
The current `description: String` field works well

. Leave it alone.

Which is it?

Good. Description stays as the immutable problem statement.

---

### Question
What happens
to existing
issues
with `
notes:
Some("
` when
you migrate
) Auto
-migrate
The single
note becomes
the
first (
and only
) entry in the new `notes: Vec<Note>` field. Its `created_at
` is set to the issue's `updated_at` (best approximation

of when it was last modified).
- **B) Move to description
** — If `notes` was set, append it to `description
` with a separator. The new `notes` field starts empty.
- **C) Orphan** — Keep the old `notes` value
in a separate field like `legacy_notes: Option<String>` for backward compatibility

, but don't populate the new `notes` array.

My recommendation:
**(A) Auto-migrate** — Preserve the data in the new
structure. Using `updated_at` as the timestamp is the best approximation
available. This gives users a clean migration path without data loss.
The migration would be: read old JSONL → transform

→ write new JSONL. One-time operation.

Which is it?

Auto-migrate. Good.

---

### Question 8

Let's talk about the data model.

Here's the proposed structure:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]

`

Should `notes` be:

- **A) `Vec<Note>`** — Always a

vector, possibly empty. Simple, consistent.
- **B) `Option<Vec<Note>>`
** — Distinguish between "no notes field" (None) and "notes field exists
but empty" (Some([])). This matters for JSONL compatibility with old files

that don't have the field.

My recommendation: **(A) `Vec<Note>`
** — Simpler. Default to empty vector for missing field using `#[serde(default
)]`. The distinction between "missing" and "empty" doesn't carry semantic weight —

both mean "no notes".

This also means JSONL files without a
`notes` field (old format) deserialize correctly as `notes: vec![]`.
Which is it?

`Vec<Note>` with `#[serde(default)]`. Clean.

---

### Question 9

What's the sorting order for notes?

- **A) Chronological
ascending** — Oldest first, newest
last.
This reads like a story: "First I did this, then I discovered

that, then I fixed it."
- **B) Reverse chronological**
— Newest first, oldest last. This shows the most recent context immediately, like

a chat log or activity feed.

My recommendation: **(A) Chronological ascending
** — This matches the "narrative" mental model. When you're
reviewing an issue, you want to understand the journey from problem → solution. Reading oldest

→ newest tells that story in order.

Reverse chronological is better for activity feeds where you only
care about "what's new". But `rivets show` is about understanding the full context

.

Which is it?

Chronological ascending. Story order.

---

### Question 10

What about JSON output (`--

json` flag)?

Currently `rivets show <id> --json` outputs the

entire `Issue` struct as JSON. With the new model:

- **A
) Direct serialization** — `notes` becomes an array of `{ "
content": "...", "created_at": "..." }` objects. Straightforward,

matches the Rust struct.
- **B) Flattened strings** — For backwards
compatibility with scripts parsing the old `notes: Option<String>`, keep
the old `notes` field as a concatenated string, and add a new `note
_history: Vec<Note>` field. This preserves existing integr

ations.

My recommendation: **(A) Direct serialization** — The JSON output should
reflect the actual data model. Scripts that were parsing `notes` as a string will
need to adapt, but this is a reasonable breaking change for a feature addition
. The old model was limited; the new model is strictly more expressive.
We're pre-1.0 (or early enough) that JSON
API stability isn't a binding constraint. Better to have clean JSON than a

compatibility shim.

Which is it?

Direct serialization. Clean JSON.

---

### Question 11

Should there be a limit on note count or content

length?

- **A) No limits** — Let users add as many notes as they want,
as long as they want. The JSONL file grows, but that's what git is for

.
- **B) Limit note count** — Cap at some reasonable number (e.g.,

100 notes per issue) to prevent runaway growth.
- **C) Limit note length
** — Cap individual note content length (e.g., 10,000 characters) but allow unlimited

count.
- **D) Both limits** — Cap both count and length.

My recommendation
: **(A) No limits** — Trust the user. If someone is adding 500
notes of 50KB each, that's their repo to manage. Notes are text, not
binary blobs. A 500-note issue is still smaller than a typical test

fixture.

If you want validation, add a warning at some threshold ("
this issue has 50+ notes, consider summarizing") but don't enforce
a hard cap. Limits create edge cases (what happens when you try to add note

101?) that aren't worth the complexity.

Which is it?

No limits. Trust the user.

---

### Question 12

How should the MCP server

handle notes?

The MCP server exposes tools for AI assistants. Currently there's likely

a tool to update issues. With multiple notes:

- **A) Single
tool, append behavior** — The existing `update_issue` tool's `notes

` parameter appends. Same as CLI.
- **B) Dedicated tool
** — Add a new `add_note` tool specifically for adding notes,

separate from `update_issue`.
- **C) Both** — `update_issue
` appends notes, but also add `add_note` as a convenience

for the common case.

My recommendation: **(B) Dedicated tool** —
AI assistants benefit from focused, single-purpose tools. An `add_note(issue
_id, content)` tool is clearer than "pass notes to update_issue,

but know it appends rather than replaces."

This also lets
the MCP tool have a simpler signature without all the other update fields
. And it documents intent: "the AI is adding a

note" vs "the AI is updating the issue."

Which is it?

Dedicated `add_note` MCP tool. Clear intent.

---

### Question 13

What

should the Note struct be called in the domain model?

- **A) `Note`**

— Simple, obvious. Used in the proposal so far.
- **B) `Comment

`** — Matches common issue tracker terminology (GitHub comments, Jira comments).
- **C
) `Entry`** — More neutral, avoids the "comment" baggage of

being conversational between users.
- **D) `LogEntry`** — Emphas

izes the chronological log aspect.

My recommendation: **(A) `Note`** — You
already use `notes` in the CLI and user-facing docs. `Note` matches
. "Comment" implies a conversation (someone replies to a comment); notes
are monologic entries. "Entry" and "LogEntry" are unnecessarily bureaucratic

.

`Note` is short, clear, and already the established term in this codebase.
Which is it?

`Note` it is.

---

### Question 14

Let's pin
down the terminology for the domain glossary. I want to

make sure we have precise definitions:

**Current terms

in the codebase:**
- `description` — The problem statement

, set at issue creation
- `design` — Design notes /

technical approach (optional)
- `acceptance_criteria` — What

"done" looks like (optional)
- `notes` —
(currently single string, becoming `Vec<Note>`
Keep all four fields distinct** — `description`, `design`, `
acceptance_criteria`, and `notes` each serve different purposes.

No overlap.
- **B) Merge `design` and `notes`**
— Both are running technical context. `design` is just the first "

design note" in the log.
- **C) Merge `acceptance_criteria
` into `notes`** — AC is just another note with a specific purpose.
My recommendation: **(A) Keep all four fields distinct** — Each field

has a different owner and lifecycle:
  - `description` — Immutable, set

at creation. "What is the problem?"
  - `design` — Semi
-mutable, updated as the approach evolves. "How will we solve it?"
- `acceptance_criteria` — Semi-mutable, refined as we learn. "How

do we know it's done?"
  - `notes` — Append-only log. "

What happened during the work?"

Merging them loses this distinction. `design
` and `acceptance_criteria` are first-class fields because they're important enough
to surface prominently in `rivets show` and query independently.
Which is it?
